title: Evergreen Review

content.question: When did you last clean out notes, tasks, or files?
sop: Run weekly inbox zero, every captured item gets routed, not left floating
Do a monthly review pass across notes, tasks, and files
Rate the maintenance level and relevance of your system honestly (see calculation, page 6)
Tag or flag items that need revisiting instead of letting them go stale silently
Archive or delete anything that's lost relevance instead of letting it accumulate as clutter
content.explainer: Relevance alone doesn't keep a system useful, a note can still be true and still be impossible to find or act on if it hasn't been maintained. Utility comes from both at once: the information has to still matter, and the system has to still be tended, or the two multiply down toward zero instead of up.
questions: When did you last clean out notes, tasks, or files?
How many open tasks or notes in your system are more than 3 months old and still unresolved?
If you had to justify keeping every note in your system today, which ones couldn't you defend?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Maintenance 3 (rarely reviewed), Relevance 8 (content still matters). Utility = 3 times 8 = 24, low, because neglect is dragging down otherwise-good content. Raise Maintenance to 8: Utility = 8 times 8 = 64. Higher utility means your system stays usable and trustworthy; lower means even relevant information becomes effectively lost.
content.closing_statement: Even good content rots without maintenance.

caption:
Principle Nº 225: Evergreen Review

Refresh second brain periodically. Relevance alone doesn't keep a system useful, a note can still be true and still be impossible to find or act on if it hasn't been maintained.

Ask yourself: When did you last clean out notes, tasks, or files?
